speaker: interviewee
text: This is tape number four. Today is February 18, 1996. My name is Judith Ehrenfeld, and I'm interviewing Mr. Harry Klein. OK, Harry. You were telling me about--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were assigned to a different barrack, the-- I mean, we were taken by truck to a barracks in a different camp, which was pretty clean. And to our amazement, some older prisoners came in to treat us. They may have been medics before they entered the camps. And-- and they said to us, don't worry, we'll save everybody. And we also have doctors. First they came in to wash the wounds. All of us were injured. And-- and later on, they-- they took-- they took-- those that had to be taken to the infirmary, or whatever they  call it, the hospital, were taken there. Like, I was taken there, and a doctor took out my bullet from my leg. Maybe with primitive instruments, but he did take it out. And they had some sort of medication to put around it, bandaged, and then I was transferred back to my barracks. Later on, they brought, the first time in eight months, good soup. I don't know exactly how they got a hold of it. And we already know the war is going to be over soon. We didn't know the exact day or the hour. We-- we-- we could see it already. It's the end. And then, as far as I'm concerned-- as far as I remember, we-- we sent-- we sent two people who were not injured badly to look around at what's going on in the camp. So they went to the-- to the front, to the office building. And he came back a half hour later and he says, there's nobody there. There's no SS guards. There's nobody there. They escaped, apparently. They already knew, and they were afraid the Allies were going to kill each one of them. And there was nobody there. And-- and they said, and there is a warehouse full of food. And we will-- we will go there in a little while, and we'll bring food. But we ask everybody not to eat too much because your stomachs are not used to food. And this is a fact. This is after liberation, two days later, which I remember-- I don't remember the exact names. Some people went in-- those ones that are in good shape, they went into villages or towns, to German homes. And the Germans opened the doors. They didn't resist. And they said to them, take all the food you want. So they did, some of them. A day later, they died because their stomachs were not used to food. Anyway, they-- they said, we'll give you food, but eat very little of it. And they didn't bring too much. They said, we have enough food there. Don't worry. And-- and so we already saw the reading on the wall that it's a matter or a day or two or there, and we will be liberated. One day later, the middle of the night, we heard singing and dancing. All of a sudden everybody was up. And-- and they said, we are free. The prisoners said, we're free. Two, three hours later, we saw American soldiers in uniforms, throwing candy and chocolate. [LAUGHS] So-- and then the next day, and they-- they brought in groups of-- not groups. Every-- every command had doctors and nurses, and they examined everybody on the spot. And they assigned, and they said, you go to this hospital. And the-- and the Germans had field hospitals. And they occupied it. And they assigned people for treatment. Most people needed treatment. I was eating rice and milk for a month. That's all they gave me, rice and milk every day. And I was examined once a week by a doctor, under nurse's supervision. And they, I have to say, they did a great job. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So after six weeks, they released me. And it was me and another two prisoners that got acquainted in the camps, and we-- we wrote these notes right after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you tell me their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't have them in the-- in the note. If I had the names, I would read it. I don't have the names. I know it was written-- it was not written by me. I didn't remember all the details. I remembered many details. But the three of us-- I said to Elizabeth, we can't be wrong. The three of us can't be wrong. [INAUDIBLE]. The same goes for Dr. Mengele. They say he was the only one. He is the only one who will be in my book. Dr. Mengele directed the people to right or left. But it doesn't say. So it says SS officers. So it was more than one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Who wrote-- who wrote the book?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mine. It's my handwriting. My handwriting in Hungarian.

speaker: interviewee
text: And would you tell me about the book, please? How long is it, and in what language?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is written in Hungarian. It has 87 pages, from day one in Ungvar to the last day in Landsberg, until liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was the experience-- your experience and these two other men?

speaker: interviewee
text: The two other men who were with me from day one in the ghetto to the last day of liberation, which is some experience. Many of them didn't make it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you have the only copy?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have the only copy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you don't know their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct. No, I do not know the names. I'm surprised I didn't write down their names right away. I should have. All right, do you want to know anything after what happened? OK. I stayed in Landsberg until-- until the-- the doctors released me. They said, you are on your own.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't remember whether they gave us money. So I don't remember how I wound up in Pocking. This is a DP camp on the German-Austrian border. It was a big DP camp. How I got there with whose money or with tickets, I don't remember. These details I don't remember. But I was there about six months, and I was recuperating and-

speaker: interviewee
text: What was the DP camp like?

speaker: interviewee
text: DP camp life, they had there a kitchen, and they-- they fed people. And-- and they had people from America, Red Cross or Joint. And they made up lists of-- took details of relatives and dates and names and locations to try to unite people with their relatives. That was the main-- in those months, that was the main purpose in these DP camps, and not only in Pocking. And I stayed there for a while and relaxed and had enough food. I don't know about clothes. I don't remember-- I had clothes on. I don't know from whom, maybe the Joint. The Joint was very active, Joint Distribution Committee. They sent loads of clothes to-- to these DP camps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And packages. They also gave packages to each person. A package could consist of a bar of chocolate and a can of sardines, which was some-- something in those years. And bread we had locally. And-- and then one day, I got transferred to Heidenheim. Whether I got transferred or I volunteered to go to the other DP camp, I don't remember. Heidenheim was a nice camp, a small camp with cottages. Instead of one big building-- a barracks-- it resembled private homes. And everybody cooked for themself. They were assigned food or rations or whatever. And I was teaching Hebrew in that camp. I was in that camp for four years, to-- to the end, till I left for America, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] almost four years. So I left for America-- I forget, in April of '49.

speaker: interviewee
text: So for those four years--

speaker: interviewee
text: Three and a half years. Three and a half years, I-- I was teaching Hebrew four or five hours a day in a Hebrew school, which is for the DP children-- for the DP camp children. And then I studied electronics in a-- in a-- in an ORT school, which was conducted by-- by ORT instructors in Germany. And I also worked in a electronics store in-- in a German store, in the town. Apparently, I knew German. I couldn't have-- where I learned German I don't know. I must have learned  it on the spot. I must have been good in German. Not today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now you don't know German today?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I can read a little. I can understand some of it, but I cannot communicate very well. But I probably could get along.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you left the DP camp during the day to work in this German store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No, no. I did my job in the Hebrew school. We didn't have to work eight hours. And then I studied-- in a-- in the German-- in the store, I didn't work every day. Two, three days a week to get practical experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: What was it like for a young Jewish man to be working in a German store? Who ran the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was a German owner, but the Germans, in-- and that was in-- in those years, they didn't talk about the camp. They didn't talk about the-- the Gestapo. I was not involved. Nobody was involved. I have no relatives. [INAUDIBLE] If you ask them, they all denied it. There was the official word, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't mention, and they didn't mention-- they definitely didn't mention the Gestapo. No, God forbid. I wasn't there. My relatives were not there. Who were they? We don't know. That's the standard-- That was the standard form of talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I stayed there. And then I applied for immigration to America. And this-- in Stuttgart, there was an American consul in Stuttgart, which was probably 100 kilometers from there, maybe less. 50 kilometers, 70 miles. And once in a while, he called me over, and we need more papers, more original documents, original birth certificate. My town, [INAUDIBLE], was Russia. Russia wouldn't give out anything. And you couldn't go for it. At that time, you went to Russia, you never returned. It was a one-way trip.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got rejected once and got rejected twice. So I had two choices. Go to Israel or go to-- to Australia. Australia didn't-- didn't require so many papers and so many details. So-- but we had [HEBREW] from Israel. All the Hebrew schools were supervised by men, teachers from Israel. They were called [HEBREW] delegates. [HEBREW] is a delegate in Hebrew.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- one guy was very honest with me. He said to me, you went through hell. And if you think that in Israel now-- I don't know what it's going to be in 20 years-- you'll have a good life or an easy life, forget it. He explained-- he drew a picture of daily life. And I said to myself, I got time for that. So I registered for Australia. When the Australian government or the consul notified me that, in about two months, you will be able to leave, all of a sudden, I got a note from the-- the American consul in Stuttgart that you're-- you're approved.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's it. I was approved, and I took the trip from British zone. And we were going on a boat. And I was sick seven days, I believe. It was a transport that I wasn't used to. So I lived on oranges for seven days.

speaker: interviewee
text: I landed in New York. And-- and the [INAUDIBLE] people-- [INAUDIBLE] people were there right at the-- at a-- at the harbor, or whatever. And they took us to-- I think they had their own big building in Manhattan, downtown someplace, probably for a day. And they- they gave us instructions, and they helped us with money and clothes. And then they assigned-- since I could speak English, I took English lessons in Germany. I don't remember how I had the time to teach Hebrew and learn electronics and learn English. I don't remember all that. But I took English lessons so I could communicate with them. And they-- they assigned furnished rooms to every-- every DP person who-- who was registered with them. And I was assigned a room in the Bronx, in the east Bronx, a nice furnished room with a Jewish family. And I lived there for a number of years, for three, four years, till I got married. Then I got a job. They gave me money for three or four months, and I was looking for work. And they helped me with that too. He contacted a company, and of course, they had rackets. Each one, was he qualified for what he did, what he could do, and so on and so forth. And I got into a-- a TV company, and within a year, I was an assistant manager. I worked hard, and I stayed there for 22 years till they folded. And-- and then, I looked for another job. I was out of work for about two months. And I got another job in a closed circuit TV corporation in New York. Over there, I also worked for one year and then I become an assistant-- became an assistant manager.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after 11 years, they folded too. Japanese companies took over. They competed with them, and they couldn't keep up. So I was out of work for about three months. And I heard an ad in that Dime Savings Bank is looking for people. I said, what do I have to lose?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went down there, and they gave everybody, 50 people in-- in Garden City. And they had their own building-- not  their own building. They rented floors. And they gave these 50 people a test, and I passed the test. And I've been there for nine and a half years. And about a half a year ago or so, I this-- this Dime Savings Bank merged with Anchor Savings Bank, and I lost my job. And right now, I'm working-- and I was out of work for three months. Right now I'm working part time in Manhattan, doing clerical work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Harry, what do you think, if you were to think about it, helped you to survive the experience that you had? You said it was your shoes. Did you mean that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The shoes helped me in the winter, because people who had their shoes or wooden shoes, their-- their-- their feet froze. And once you're frozen, and how-- if you are very weak, and the cold didn't give up. It was the cold season. And I knew-- I knew-- I knew people who died because they didn't have the right shoes. And I had very heavy boots, and they were in good shape. And they say, I believe it's a part of-- while I was working, while I was active, that saved me. You know, every little bit helped.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in addition to your shoes, why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only my will, nothing else. There were many, many days I wished I was dead. Only my will. I figured I-- I went through so much, maybe, maybe a slight hope for me. And that's the only thing. And chances-- I took a chance, like staying in the barracks off so many days, when I knew that, once in a while they come with trucks, they take the people away and never see them again. I took chances. And once in a while, I was working in a field. I was marching in a field to work, and I picked up a-- a carrot or something. I got hit for it, but I had the food already in my stomach. And that helped too. And I was stealing bread from a-- in the railroad-- when I was loading railroad cars or unloading, stealing bread. I was making deals in Warsaw with civilians for bread. And it was the general idea to get a little more than what they assigned to you, because what they assigned to you was a-- It was called an Arbeit camp, and Arbeit camp means labor camp. But the-- actually, it was a-- it was a labor camp with one provision, that most of them will die in time. Because at the rate that the work was going on, against the rations they assigned, there was no way that anybody could survive for-- for half a year, unless he had some sources or stealing or begging or trading or anything he could to get a little food or clothes. Clothes helped too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is there any message that you would like to leave for your children and grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would like to leave a message for them. However bad things are, they could be worse. And don't give up. Don't be a pessimist. But there were people that survived under-- under worst circumstances. So-- and do the best you can. That's all. Do the best you can and don't blame yourself, because that's the best you can do. But if you give up and you don't do the best you can, you're just causing it for yourself, whatever it is.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, Harry. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] OK, Harry. Tell me about the picture, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. This picture was taken approximately in 1936 in Mukacevo. The person-- the girl number two from the left, is one of my sisters. I can't identify her, and I don't know exactly her name. The old woman, which is one, two, three-- number six from the left, is my grandmother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Rachel-- Rachel. The person number two or number three from the right-- number two or number three, most likely number three-- is my father. All the other persons on the picture I cannot identify as of now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the notebook I was referring to in my previous conversation. It was written and composed by three ex-prisoners, including me, about six weeks after the war was over, which is probably the end of June, 1945. The last sentence of this book I will translate into English. It says, 'With this ended the Hitler barbaric and inhuman torture.' And then one word that says 'the end.'

speaker: interviewee
text: What language was this written in?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was written in Hungarian. And to my surprise, a perfect Hungarian.

speaker: interviewee
text: And how many pages is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It contains 87 pages, and all the details from day one at the ghetto in Ungvar, to the day of liberation in a camp in Landsberg, which is near Stuttgart.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, Harry.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture contains me and my sister Civja, and a friend of ours from [INAUDIBLE]. And my friend-- my sister Civja is alive in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where is your sister? Which side? Don't point. Just tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister is on the right side of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: As you look at the picture, which way-- which side of picture is she on?

speaker: interviewee
text: She is on the right side.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, Harry.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of me and my sister, Pirchia, taken in Germany after the war. This sister of mine is alive in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is of my sister, Ester. And it was probably taken in Israel after the war. She's alive in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, Harry.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture contains me on the right and my brother Yakov on the left. He's alive. He resides in Brooklyn.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Heidenheim, a DP camp, after the war. It contains teachers. They were teaching at a public school in Heidenheim. I'm number two on the right hand side, top row.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the sign on the top of the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This sign on the top says Hebrew Public School Heidenheim. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is Elizabeth, my wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is our grandchildren. Starting from the right is my son David and Judy's children-- Jared, and the middle is Joshua, and right here in front of me is Michael. And the little baby is Daniel. And David and Judy. And these little two children are my grandchildren of my daughter, Mona and Mark's children. And this is Sage, and this is Ethan.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just wanted to share an experience. A few years ago, my sister Mona and my wife Judy and I went to a Children of the Holocaust Survivors convention in New York City. And I remember I was talking to Mona about halfway through the day. And many of the people there were talking about their experiences, how their lives were-- were different because their parents were survivors of the Holocaust. And I remember talking to Mona and Judy and-- and specifically saying, we don't relate to these experiences, that we didn't really feel any different than any of the other kids growing up on Beach 63rd Street in Arverne. And although I think some of the people there might have been using their-- their parents' history as a-- maybe a crutch for some of their problems, my parents-- they never conveyed that to us. They came to this country, they built a home here, they built a family here. They didn't use any crutches, and they certainly didn't convey that to us. And we are who we are because we stand on our own feet, and they gave us that-- that kind of background. And-- and I don't think we were raised any differently than-- than any of the other kids that we grew up with. And I think that's a real-- real tribute to my parents and their effort in-- in raising us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Except for the part that they're really overprotective. [LAUGHTER] I think that is different than other parents. I just wanted to say that I think this is a really important project. It's a legacy for our family. And even though I was raised in a home where I was always aware of what happened to my parents, I was not necessarily aware of it in any kind of chronology, with a start and a middle and a finish. There were details, but the details were not necessarily strung along in a continuum. And this really gives us the ability to have that perspective forever, and for our children to have it forever, exactly, you know what happened and how it happened and how they survived. And it's a wonderful thing. It's a gift for us to be able to have done this. So I thank all of you for spending all day here. And I thank my parents for doing what they did for us.
